content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 10, 2006

John St. Croix, Executive Director
Ethics Commission – City & County
of San Francisco
30 Van Ness Avenue, Suite 3900
San Francisco, CA 94102-6027

Re: Your Request for Advice
File No. W-05-257

Dear Mr. St. Croix:

You have requested reconsideration of the advice provided in the Donovan
Advice Letter, our File No. A-05-207. That letter concerned the application of provisions
of the San Francisco Campaign Finance Reform Ordinance to Ms. Donovan's client in
light of parallel sections in the Political Reform Act.

Although we are very sympathetic to the city's interest in this advice, your letter
seeks reconsideration of advice regarding the duties of another – the requestor in the
Donovan Advice Letter. As you may be aware, the Fair Political Practices Commission
does not provide third party advice. In part, the Commission will not consider a third
party's request to reconsider the advice to another because it may require consideration of
facts not provided by the third party. (Section 83114(b); regulation 18329(b)(8), copy
enclosed.) This may lead to the analysis of disputed facts and debates among those
parties about the legal analysis the Commission staff should employ. Since Commission
advice provides immunity only if the underlying facts are correct, it generally does not
result in problems for governmental entities. Based on the Commission's current policies
and practices, reconsideration of the advice letter is not appropriate.

However, as you are aware, any person may request the Commission to issue an
opinion on a matter, so long as the request satisfies the requirements of section 83114(a)
and regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). This may be an appropriate avenue for you to
pursue, in light of issues you raise concerning the interaction of the state law and your
local ordinance. In the past, the Commission has issued opinions to local government
entities concerning issues of preemption. This also gives the Commission as a whole the

File No. W-05-257
Page No. 2

opportunity to consider the issue, as advice letters are generated by staff and are not
reviewed by the Commission.

If you have questions concerning this letter, or still believe a meeting would be
helpful prior to your submission of an opinion request, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

Liane Randolph
Chairman

cc: Mark Krausse
Luisa Menchaca

sections.question_synthetic: Should the FPPC reconsider its prior advice (File No. 4-05-207) regarding the application of San Francisco Campaign Finance Reform ordinance provisions to a client in light of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to reconsider advice provided to another party, as it does not issue third-party advice per Section 83114(a) and Regulation 18329(d)(5). However, the requestor may pursue an opinion request under Section 83114(a) and Regulation 18320 if they wish the Commission to address the preemption issues between state and local law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC response declining reconsideration request. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections with headers, but clear advisory content present. File No. W-05-257 indicates this is a withdrawal or declination type. Date is January 10, 2006, though document ID lists 2005.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should the FPPC reconsider its prior advice () regarding the application of San Francisco Campaign Finance Reform ordinance provisions to a client in light of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declines to reconsider advice provided to another party, as it does not issue third-party advice per Section 83114(a) and Regulation 18329(d)(5). However, the requestor may pursue an opinion request under Section 83114(a) and Regulation 18320 if they wish the Commission to address the preemption issues between state and local law.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines a request to reconsider prior advice (the Donovan letter) regarding San Francisco's Campaign Finance Reform ordinance, explaining that it does not provide third-party advice but suggesting the requestor pursue an opinion request instead.
